This oral history is a part of the America 250 celebration with support from the Idaho Historical Society, the oral history project of which this interview is a part, supports the commemorations mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, equality, liberty and justice through meaningful community engagement and recognize, appreciate and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. First, let me thank you, Mr. Roser, for your time today. I hope that this interview will allow you to provide your thoughts and insights about the Caldwell Night Rodeo, as well as your memories and experiences with it. These questions aim to understand your personal experiences with the radio, as well as how it developed over your life so that future generations can hear your thoughts. We'll begin with some general questions to get started and moving to more specific topics from there. The order of the questions may change as we talk, and I may ask some unscripted questions. How did you get started there? I know you said that it's kind of just a family tradition, but. And, and it's a way big, you know, I mean, like the offices in, in Colorado Springs and stuff, they, they have a lot of employees and everything. And it's, you know, it's just gotten bigger and bigger, I don't know. Back in the really early days, I think there was only 30 some members that formed the association. And, you know, it's just it's grown just like every other aspect of sports, you know. I mean, back in the old days when, when football didn't even have when they had leather helmets, you know, I mean, everything evolves and it just it goes for the better. Sorry. I've just lost my train of thought. Um. So, I mean, you said you still occasionally participate in rodeos. Did you wear a tie down Roper? It's actually steer roping. Thank you. Um, and what what it is, is it's similar to like tie down roping. Steer roping. You rope a bigger steer and you. You put a trip around them and trip them to the ground and then tie them. And like a lot of rodeos in Texas, Oklahoma on in through their habit and a lot of them in Oregon and Washington have it, but it's not so much around here. Um, but I like last year I, I only went to six rodeos of that and Emmett has it and, uh, they just started doing it. They've only had it two years, but rodeos like the Pendleton Round-Up has it. And, and, uh, like I go to Prineville and Union, Oregon and stuff like that. And Joseph Oregon and, um, I, you know, I just do that just because you don't have to worry about finding a, a partner and the team roping is so competitive now I don't, I don't, uh, rope as good as I used to when I was younger. And so I, I would, I wouldn't be very competitive in the team open and actually in the steer roping. The best thing for me is they have the legacy steer roping, and it's a separate event for guys that are over 50 and haven't been to the like the national finals steer roping in the last three years. It's for the older guys and I enjoy that part of it because I can be competitive in that and I just do it, you know, because I love to be around it and stuff, and it's just something I like to do. You said they didn't do that. The Caldwell Night Rodeo. Do you have any idea why. They never have? Um, there's it's seems to be that an area will have it and then more of them will, will get to do it. And you know, it takes a lot more cattle and everything. And, and sometimes it might be based on a stock contractor that doesn't want to mess with it or what for whatever reason, but they've, they've never had it at Caldwell. And there's very few rodeos in Idaho. Salmon had it for a little while. And then, um, the guy that is the main guy at Emmett, he's, he's done a really good job with that rodeo. That rodeo has really grown. And he told me three years ago, he said, we're having steer roping at Emmett next year. And I said, that'd be so cool. And a lot of times, a lot of times it'll be in the slack. You know what the slack is where they have extra. They have too many contestants for the performance. So they have an extra session that's slack and and it's mostly in the timed event, but a lot of times the steer roping will be in the slack, you know? So sounds like you're on a pretty good like name, name, first name basis with a lot of people around, not just Caldwell Night Rodeo, but others. Is that. Yeah, true. Do you find that's like that's common amongst members of the rodeo community or. Yeah. You know, I mean, they always people will always say, you know, well, you know, like rodeo is one big family. And, you know, like I like Randy Corley, the announcer Caldwell. And he's announced that rodeo for right close to 40 years. Well, I met him when we lived in California. He was just starting to announce rodeos and stuff. And, you know, I mean, I've known him for almost 40 years, you know, and, you know, like, I know a lot of the announcers and things personally and, and then, you know, like when I was going to rodeos, there were stock contractors like Cotton Rosser that he passed away a couple years ago, like 93 years old. Well, he, he like knew my dad really well. And, and so then, you know, there it is going back to the family thing again. Oh, you know, Jim was your dad, you know. And and so you just get to know it everybody. And it just, you know. I mean, that's to me, that's the thrill of it is, is everybody, you know, and stuff like that. A lot, a lot of the kids that I went to college with, like in Pendleton went to the NFR and things. And it's just, it's just fun, you know? I mean, with, you know, Idaho's growth and just the growth of everywhere in general. Are you, are there any fears about like going away with rodeo, that sense of community or. You know, they, they passed a thing here recently about that. They outlawed rodeo in LA County. Well, that's not something that people should just say, well, it's LA, you know, to heck with them because that's where those kind of things start. But I mean, it would be, it would be pretty hard to for them to do that kind of stuff. And sometimes the, you know, the tree hugger type people, they, you know, that's their goal. But, but, uh, I think it's so, it's so big and it's, and it's so, you know, you take states like Wyoming and Idaho and, and stuff like that, that they're not going to stand for that, you know, but you got to be careful though, because it can start somewhere like that. But, you know, and I mean, they've, they've proved time and time again that those horses in the bucking events, that the flank straps are easy on them. They're bred to buck. You're born to buck is what they say. And you know, they're not horses that, you know, when they. It's just like a, a hunting dog. He he's got hunting, you know, they want to point and stuff like that. And it's in their blood. And a like border collie is is a sheep or cattle. They want to work them. That's in their blood. And the horses, you know, they're born to buck and they won't buck unless they like it, you know. So, you know, I mean, you, a lot of times they, they've proved over and over that, you know, their, their assessments that oh, it's hard on livestock or, you know, I mean, it's just not true really. But you know, I mean, I, I hope that it never does go anywhere, but you never know, I guess. And it sounds like then that sort of challenged rodeo from those sort of groups is relatively common or well. It can be. Um, but I think more and more of those groups are finding out that it's, it would be such an uphill battle. When I was in California, We went to a rodeo. Me and another guy in Guerneville in Guerneville is up in the redwood trees. And a lot of like what they always called back in them days. A lot of the hippies lived up there. Well, there was when we came back to the vehicle after the rodeo, they had went around and, and stuck little propaganda things in everybody's windshield wiper. And, you know, and I mean, most of them just got thrown away in the garbage, you know, which is where they needed to be. But, you know, I mean, that that was in the 80s, you know, so I mean, there's always going to be that somebody that there's always going to be be people that, that, you know, I mean, they can't just mind their own business and, and be happy. They have to be doing some ridiculous thing like that or they're not happy. I mean, I just assumed everybody does their own thing in life to try to make themselves happy. But. You've mentioned, you know, with the increase in prizes, that the sort of intensity of the competition has increased a lot since you started. Yeah. You know, like, like this year, this year, Caldwell's going to add $31,000 per event for prize money. And then the entry fees go in that. And that's what makes the payoff. And, um, you know, back in the days when it wasn't very much added money, of course, everything's, you know. The money that that you won back in those days wasn't a lot, you know, but, um, but you know, gas wasn't very much either, you know, and stuff. So everything's higher. but it's like they set records every year now. For the money one to be like the world champion and those records every year. You know, and they like Las Vegas events adds more money into the deal every year. Seems like and it it brings out more interest in it, you know, because they're like. You know, kids that were, were like, yeah, I think I'd like to do that because they can see how much money these guys are winning. And so then that brings out more people, you know. And, and like I say, a lot of them are, are not necessarily like ranch type people. They'll, you know, they'll, it'll bring them out there and they, there's tons of schools they can go to to learn and stuff like that. And it just, I think it's just evolving. It's gotten bigger and bigger. And, you know, I mean, if you think back a few years ago, you couldn't hardly find a rodeo on TV and now they're on every night. So it's, it's just evolving. You know. It sounds like you said the you couldn't find rodeo if you didn't actually on TV. You said it's that's how is in your experience has. I mean, obviously, you know, it's easier to find now, but has that affected any other kind of media or. Oh, I'm sure it does. But, um, you know, I mean, you, you take a store like, like D and B, you know, the week before the rodeo, they sell a lot of wranglers and cowboy hats and boots and stuff like that. And, and it's because people are going to go to the rodeo and they want to, you know, kind of fit in. And so it, you know, I think it all makes a big effect on everything. And, you know, like I say, you couldn't, um, it used to be back in the 80s or something. You got to watch. Maybe the final performance of the NFR. And then they started televising all ten performances. And, you know, like now with the cowboy channel and, and the guy that started the cowboy channel that Patrick Gosch and he passed away a couple years ago. But, um, you know, he had a vision that, that it's just going to get bigger and bigger. And, and so now, you know, they, there's rodeos on every night. That's about all I watch on TV is the cowboy channel because I keep, I keep track, you know, like mental notes and stuff. Um, you know who's doing good here and there and stuff and, and, you know, like, and like I say, I, I still know a lot of the contestants. One of my roommates from college, his daughter has has won $9,000 at Houston already, and she'll be in the finals tomorrow night. So, you know, I mean, that's that's awesome. That's and I know her because I went to Arizona and, and wrote down there for three years and, and I got to know her pretty well. And because he, her dad was one of my roommates in college. So it's, it's just, it's really cool to, you know, you watch that and people that, you know, and stuff. And I've, I've sold a couple horses to guys that, that the horses did good in, in rodeo and they, and they won money on them. And you know, and that's, that's fun to, you know, both of those horses are retired out of it now because it's been a few years, but it's fun to have have that kind of happen, that stuff. And. I mean, with the, you know, raising intensity of it. Do you think the number I mean, it's an intense sport. So the injuries gotten better or worse with changes? Well, the, um, you know, like with the, the rough stock injuries and things, um, I think they've gotten better because they take more precautions. Like when I was a bareback rider, I taped my wrist with white athletic tape and I put what was a, a little shin guard from soccer, a little plastic because it's, it puts enough pressure on this bone here that that bone would actually kind of bend while you were riding. And, but today, and I didn't take my elbow at all today. If you look at one of those bareback riders, they've got tape all the way up here and and it crosses here and they have all kinds of things and their arm can't even really straighten. So it doesn't hurt their elbow because it the tape won't let it straighten. Warned about that. But it's just that they've learned over the years to take these precautions to keep from getting hurt. And, and so, and then like most all of the big rodeos have the Justin sports trailers there. And they have actual doctors that, that watch the event. So they can say, well, I saw how this, this happened, this injury happened, and they can treat them right there and things like that. And, and, uh, so that's, that's made it better. It's just like having, it's just like an NFL team having a doctor right on the sidelines. You know, if you, it's just the preventative thing on the injuries is, is way better than it used to be. And they have, you know, there's the timed events have plenty of, you know, like in the steer wrestling, like knees and things like that. And, but, um, you know, and like in the timed events, let's just say it's a steer wrestling. Well, there's the guy's friends and traveling partners and whatever else they're watching. And if, and like I say, they want to hit the barrier so that they have the, the best shot at being the fastest because it all comes down to tenths of a second. Well, they have there'll be somebody that'll say you're out, meaning that they, they got a clean shot at the barrier because if you break the barriers. 10s. And if if, if they if somebody says broke it, you know, and the contestant hears it, well, he might ride by instead of jumping the steer just to keep from having a freak injury happen. So it's, you know, I mean, that's just another part of the evolving deal. Sounds like then the cooperation is pretty important part of this. Oh it is. It's, you know, like and and sometimes I wish on the cowboy channel. They, they pointed out a lot, you know, like, like the steer wrestlers are a really tight group, you know, because they, they ride each other's horses and they haz for each other. And the guy that, that just put down the fastest run, well, he'll come back and he might jump on the Hazen horse and he's Hazen for somebody trying to beat him and and you know and it's all and, and like in the bronc riding, you'll see the guy that just rode two horses ago helping the next guy get out, you know, and, and you, you help contestants beat you, you know, and, and you're happy for them when they did, you know, I mean, your time will come around and and that's just part of it. That's part of this. The things that people involved in rodeo like so well is, is the, the people there. And you know, it's nobody's like, oh, I don't want him to know. Good. You know, it's like, okay, I did my deal. You come and beat me and that's that's good. The little guy comes in going like this. I mean. It's I mean. It's all right. That's all right. You gotta look at your notes. You see what? I mean, is somebody, you know, you're a rancher. You're out here in the rural parts of Idaho. I mean, Goryeo has its origins there. So how. In you mentioned earlier you thought rodeo was really good for America. I think it's like a. Link to that past. You know, what. Do you think rodeo can really serve as a link to help people understand. what America's past was like. I think so. Yeah, I think. I think it does. You know, because, I mean, you know, and nowadays the announcers are always referring to different things as if. And they'll ask the crowd, you know, how many people is this your first rodeo? And you'll hear some applause response, you know. And so the announcers are continually going over the rules and stuff like that. So that so that they're not confused on things. They, you know, they want to tell them how the barrier works. And you know, like they always say, you can tell whether the, the calf tripped the barrier or whether the horse hit it first, you know, and the announcers point that out. And so it's good for that, you know. Yeah. Hey, where are you going? In the Uh. I mean, earlier you mentioned, you know, there's always those people who have it out for rodeo. I mean, do you think obviously with cities becoming so much bigger, is that going to become a bigger problem in the future? I mean. I don't know, I don't see how it couldn't, but I, I, I don't know, it kind of makes you nervous, you know, but I mean, there's so many, there's so many people now and stuff that, that don't even have a clue that he even exists, you know? So I don't know, it's, it's scary, but. I don't know. When we were at the discussing with the people at the rodeo, one of them mentioned they hoped that it could serve as sort of an education tool for those city people so they could understand the importance of rodeo. How do you feel about that idea? Well, I think it can, you know, because if if somebody goes to a rodeo for the first time they've ever been at one. And like I was just saying, the announcers help you understand it. It's it's got to make it be a more of a favorable deal, you know? And you know, like, like they always say to, you know, the, these guys don't get paid a salary. They're counting on the prize money. They can win, you know. So it's, it's, you know, I mean, they don't get a salary like a football player does. So to help them understand that stuff, it's great that the announcers help them understand that. And it looks like it's about an hour coming up on an hour. So is there anything else you would like to add? Oh, I don't know. I think we pretty much covered them all. Thank you so much for your time again. It's been great being able to talk about this with you. Yeah. I, you know, I just kind of, you know, like, like Nicole. You know, she, I, I went to high school with her dad and stuff and I've known her for ever and, and, uh, you know, when I don't know, she called or you called first or whatever, but I think she, she did, but, um, you know, I, I mean, I just kind of expect those kinds of things because she knows that I'm the kind of guy that would, would do that. You know, I did a, we did a, a radio interview thing here 3 or 4 years ago and, uh, it was on the radio and, you know, it was, it was fun. And, and, you know, she just, I know Nicole's like, oh, Tim would be the guy for that deal. And, you know, and I do have a pretty extensive history of it. Yeah, I. Have. Everybody always says you're like an encyclopedia, you know, because. It drives my wife crazy because I'll be watching the Cowboy channel and one of the announcers will say something and I'll be like, that ain't right, you know? But, you know, they might have the year that somebody won a world championship off by 3 or 4 years or, or they might say this is a two time world champion. And I'll be like, he's a three time world champion because I, you know, I mean, I just I'm kind of like an encyclopedia about it. And once again, thanks for your time. And it's been a very enlightening conversation. Well, I'm glad we do it.

story.description: Hour long Interview with Tim Roeser about his personal history and his history with the Caldwell Night Rodeo.
story.title: Tim Roeser Oral History Interview
